ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class; no specific ebi tempura udon entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers udon noodle soup broadly; no specific tempura-topped variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for ebi tempura udon; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of ebi tempura udon weighs approximately 450 g, comprising udon noodles, dashi-soy broth, one or two pieces of prawn tempura, and a garnish of seaweed flakes.

nutrient_source.title: Ebi Tempura Udon — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Ebi Tempura Udon (cr_id F05060047) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for shrimp tempura and udon noodle soup components and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ebi tempura udon as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles (white wheat udon, boiled). Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 falls within the low GI category (≤55). The deep-fried tempura component adds fat which modestly attenuates postprandial glucose rise. gi_category is therefore set to 'low' consistent with the GI value of 55 and the University of Sydney classification boundary. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (10.31 g/100g × 450 g serving = 46.4 g) ÷ 100 = 25.5 g. Analogous estimate documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 297 mg/100 g; a full 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1,337 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg, with additional sodium from the dashi-soy broth if fully consumed.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid drinking the broth, request reduced-sodium soy sauce, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Udon noodles are a refined wheat carbohydrate with an estimated GI of 55 (low-medium boundary); a standard 450 g bowl delivers approximately 46 g available carbohydrate with only 4.2 g dietary fibre, producing a glycaemic load of 25.5 g per serving that elevates postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl, avoid consuming the full broth, and consider substituting udon with a lower-GI noodle such as soba to reduce glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried tempura batter contributes 4.65 g fat/100 g (estimated 0.7 g saturated fat/100 g) and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); combined with high sodium, this dish presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request grilled or steamed prawn instead of tempura, avoid the full broth to limit sodium, and consume this dish only occasionally as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried tempura generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation; refined udon noodles contribute glycaemic-load-driven systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried tempura preparations and request grilled or steamed prawn topping with reduced-sodium broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ebi tempura udon (海老天ぷらうどん) is a classic Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of thick, soft, chewy white wheat udon noodles served in a hot dashi-based broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, topped with one or two pieces of ebi (prawn) tempura — deep-fried prawns coated in a light, crispy wheat-and-egg batter — and garnished with aonori (dried seaweed flakes). The dish originates from Japan and is widely served in Japanese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The broth is umami-rich and savoury; the udon noodles are smooth and filling; the tempura provides a contrasting crispy texture. Per 100 g, the dish provides 93 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 4.65 g fat, and 10.31 g carbohydrate, with 297 mg sodium — reflecting the sodium-dense dashi-soy broth. The high sodium content (approximately 1,337 mg per 450 g bowl) makes this dish a concern for hypertensive patients. The udon noodle base has an estimated GI of 55 (low boundary) with a glycaemic load of 25.5 g per serving, making portion control important for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-fried tempura component generates AGEs and adds saturated fat, raising moderate cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis concerns. Allergens include wheat, crustaceans, soy, egg, and fish (from dashi stock). This dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal-certified in most preparations due to mirin (rice wine) content.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id F05060047) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite for comparable tempura udon components and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database udon noodle entry as no direct composite GI measurement exists; estimation basis fully documented. gi_category corrected to 'low' consistent with GI value of 55 per University of Sydney classification boundary (low ≤55).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for shrimp tempura and udon noodle soup components, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent ebi tempura udon entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database udon noodle entry; no direct GI measurement for ebi tempura udon as a composite dish exists in approved sources. gi_category is set to 'low' per University of Sydney GI classification (low GI ≤55); GI 55 sits at the exact boundary and is classified as low. Glycemic load per serving = 55 × 46.4 g ÷ 100 = 25.5 g. 4. The input record incorrectly classified this dish as 'western' cuisine; corrected to 'Japanese' based on dish origin and composition. 5. Halal status is uncertain as traditional mirin contains alcohol; is_halal flagged as false pending halal-certified preparation confirmation. 6. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for ebi tempura udon in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16. 7. Iteration 3 fix: gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55, which falls at the low/medium boundary and is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database standard (low ≤55). glycemic_load_per_serving corrected from 32.0 to 25.5 to reflect accurate calculation: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (46.4 g) ÷ 100.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification, low GI is defined as ≤55; GI 55 therefore falls in the low category. This resolves the q4 failure reported by the validator.
Iteration 3: Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving from 32.0 to 25.5 to reflect accurate calculation: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (10.31 g/100g × 450 g = 46.4 g) ÷ 100 = 25.5 g.
Iteration 3: Updated gi_source source_match_notes to explicitly state the University of Sydney GI classification boundary justification for gi_category 'low' at GI 55.
Iteration 3: Updated health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reference corrected GL of 25.5 g per serving.
Iteration 3: Updated confidence_reason to document gi_category correction.
